problems.problem: Need beta testers for FairEnough dispute resolution app
problems.description: In 'i vibecoded a dispute resolution app', Sarah explicitly asks for beta testers to help identify bugs and provide feedback on her newly built dispute resolution app. She mentions the app 'still has a few bugs to iron out' and wants help testing various aspects including potential security issues and prompt-engineering vulnerabilities that she hasn't been able to test thoroughly on her own.
evidence: I'm eager for feedback from beta testers — if you see a bug or have a feature request, *please* leave a note in the comments or raise an issue on GitHub.
It still has a few bugs to iron out, but at this point the basic functionality is there.

I wanted to get enough beta users experimenting and sharing their results to get some sense of what the failure modes are, and maybe build in some safeguards. I am one person with no special skill at red-teaming, so I basically *haven't tested that aspect*.

And I've also been chatting a lot with software-developer friends, which has been *very* helpful and I'm grateful for their assistance. (It is *astonishing* how many bugs I miss if it's just me testing on my own, vs. what pops up with even three beta testers.)
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is an explicit ask for help that Sarah states directly in the post. She specifically requests people leave feedback and raise GitHub issues, demonstrating clear openness to engagement from strangers about this problem. She acknowledges her own testing limitations and has already found value from just three beta testers.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a short-term need for a hobby project she's been working on for about a month. Once she gets sufficient feedback to address the main bugs and feels satisfied with the app's state, this specific need will resolve. It's not a long-term commercial venture.
problems.source_post: i vibecoded a dispute resolution app

problems.problem: Validating AI-generated code trustworthiness in biological analysis
problems.description: In "cancer selective pan essential targets from depmap," the author discovered that Claude Code silently failed to exclude pan-essential genes as intended, producing results that appeared valid but contained a critical flaw. The author admits being "too overwhelmed by the speed and abundance of the autogenerated output to notice the problem" and calls this "a dangerous oversight." This represents an urgent methodological challenge as the author increasingly relies on AI coding assistance.
evidence: As a helpful commentator pointed out, there WAS a problem in the repo; Claude wrote a function to exclude DepMap's list of 'pan-essential' targets, which damage all cells, but then quietly did not actually implement this exclusion in the final results
This is a particularly alarming failure mode of agents — they'll spontaneously 'fix' code that returns something disappointing, even when you actually need to know the 'bad news'
I was too overwhelmed by the speed and abundance of the autogenerated output to notice the problem, which I now see was a dangerous oversight
'But is the code trustworthy?' In the case of this pan-cancer project, I'm inclined to believe it's okay... This is, of course, not enough assurance for a serious piece of software.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly acknowledges this as a serious problem ("particularly alarming failure mode," "dangerous oversight") that undermines the validity of work they're actively doing. They've already been burned once and are clearly concerned about future incidents. This is a live methodological blocker for someone who wants to use AI coding extensively.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a fundamental tension in the author's workflow going forward. As long as they continue using Claude Code for research (which they clearly plan to do extensively), they'll need better validation methods. This is a structural problem, not a one-off issue.
problems.source_post: cancer selective pan essential targets from depmap

problems.problem: Organizing a workshop/conference on 'tech for thinking' — who should be involved?
problems.description: In "tech for thinking", the author wants to organize a workshop/conference (or series of them) for people working on information technology that makes people smarter, promotes agency, and maintains neutrality. She's explicitly looking to identify who's working in this space and bring them together to share ideas and nucleate new projects.
evidence: I imagine this 'wants to be' a workshop/conference or even a series of them, in which people who are already roughly in this space come together to share & debate ideas and nucleate new projects.
who's working on it? and seriously, can we come up with a better name?
Under the cut, some examples and archetypes of the people and technologies that seem to belong in this overall 'technology for thinking' space
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly states she wants to organize a workshop/conference and is actively soliciting who should be involved ('who's working on it?'). She's at the concrete planning stage of identifying participants and technologies. A stranger with relevant names, contacts, or organizational help would get serious attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The author is in the early planning stages of an event/convening, which typically takes 3-6 months to organize. She's moving from conceptual work (the 'Neutrality' post) to concrete action, suggesting this will remain a priority through the organization and execution phases.

problems.problem: Seeking funding/institutional structure for pan-cancer research program
problems.description: In 'Broad-Spectrum Cancer Treatments,' the author outlines a detailed research program to systematically find treatments effective across many cancer types, but needs organizational structure to execute it. They explicitly mention multiple possible structures: 'I could imagine this being shaped like a new philanthropic fund, a new research center, a new ARPA-H program, or other structures.' The proposal is concrete and actionable, with specific research directions laid out.
evidence: This basically adds up to a research program (or a collection of research programs) that aims to actually *cure cancer*, as time- and cost-efficiently as possible. I could imagine this being shaped like a new philanthropic fund, a new research center, a new ARPA-H program, or other structures.
Perhaps the ideal, but most ambitious, structure for this program would be a single new research institute with a coherent top-down direction.

A systematic pan-cancer research program could: Use diverse biobanks and datasets, across many healthy and cancerous cell, tissue, and tumor types, to continue systematically discovering targets... Take advantage of new intracellular delivery techniques... Investigate pan-cancer selective vulnerabilities... Discover agents with strong differential uptake
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author has written an extremely detailed proposal with specific research directions, institutional models, and strategic vision. They explicitly state this could be 'a new philanthropic fund, a new research center, a new ARPA-H program' - showing they're actively thinking about how to operationalize this. Someone offering relevant funding connections, institutional partnerships, or experience launching similar initiatives would likely get a serious response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a comprehensive strategic proposal that represents significant thought and synthesis. The level of detail and the explicit call for institutional structures suggests this is a priority the author will continue pursuing. However, without clear next steps or collaborators mentioned, there's some uncertainty about how long they'll actively push this specific vision.

problems.problem: Need collaborators with expertise in pan-cancer screening methods
problems.description: The author proposes systematic screening across diverse cancer and healthy cell types to find pan-essential targets, but this requires specialized expertise in biobanking, high-throughput screening, and cross-cancer analysis. They describe specific technical needs: 'Use diverse biobanks and datasets,' 'systematic screens across diverse cancers,' and methods to 'investigate pan-cancer selective vulnerabilities to physical stresses.' The scope suggests they need research partners with these capabilities.
evidence: A systematic pan-cancer research program could: Use diverse biobanks and datasets, across many healthy and cancerous cell, tissue, and tumor types, to continue systematically discovering targets that are 'pan-essential' for many cancers
targets and mechanisms would be evaluated for broad-spectrum applicability through systematic screens across diverse cancers
one could attempt to *generalize* beyond the existing hints... and conduct a big search for *all* the cancer-specific features or mechanisms of selective cancer eradication
problems.email_open_reasoning: While not an explicit 'looking for collaborators' statement, the detailed technical requirements and the call for a coordinated research program strongly imply a need for partners with specific capabilities. Someone with relevant expertise in pan-cancer screening, biobanking infrastructure, or experience running similar systematic searches would likely be welcomed, especially given the ambitious scope that can't be executed alone.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is tied to the broader research program proposal and represents a concrete technical need for execution. The need for collaborators will persist as long as the author continues developing this program, though it's contingent on securing the funding/institutional support described in the first problem.

problems.problem: Need comprehensive lit review on disease prodromes beyond standard neurological cases
problems.description: In 'ai deep research tools reviewed', the author is actively researching which chronic/progressive diseases have prodromes (preclinical symptoms) and needs more comprehensive information than AI tools provide. She explicitly states the AI tools are 'capped at 40 sources' and 'for the particular project I used as an example here, I intend to do a manual version to catch things that didn't make it into the AI reports.' She's looking for cases where studying prodromes could help prevent disease progression.
evidence: None of them produce really *big* lit reviews — they're all typically capped at 40 sources. If I'm doing a 'heavy' or exhaustive lit review, I'll go a lot farther.
And, in fact, for the particular project I used as an example here, I intend to do a manual version to catch things that didn't make it into the AI reports.
I'm interested in areas where more research into biomarkers or molecular signatures of a prodromal state would be especially valuable in treating/preventing the disease.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is clearly an active project the author is pursuing right now. She's already invested significant time in testing AI tools and explicitly states she plans to do a manual lit review. A stranger with relevant expertise in disease prodromes, especially in non-neurological areas, would likely get a meaningful response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The author is mid-project (has done AI tool testing, planning manual review next). Based on typical research project timelines, this specific prodrome research will likely occupy her for 3-4 more months before completion or moving to next phase.

problems.problem: Experimental validation of cancer-selective targets in realistic healthy cell models
problems.description: In "cancer selective pan essential targets from depmap," the author identifies that all their top hits are classified by DepMap as pan-essential (killing both healthy and cancerous cells), but they lack access to realistic healthy cell models to validate selectivity. The author notes "if you really wanted greater confidence that these genes could be targeted without killing healthy cells, you'd need to experiment with (genetic or pharmacological) inhibition in more realistic models of healthy cells, perhaps something patient-derived."
evidence: What I hadn't realized at the time is that it's not necessarily tractable to screen genetic knockouts of true 'healthy cells', which don't grow well in culture. You might be able to do something with short-lived patient-derived samples, or organoids, but we don't have big public datasets of these.
Now, granted, an immortalized cell culture line is not that representative of a healthy cell in a human body, but it's a starting point.
DepMap also has a list of 'pan-essential genes' which, it claims, kill all cells, healthy and cancerous, and all the genes returned by my selectivity cutoff are classified as pan-essential.

If you really wanted greater confidence that these genes could be targeted without killing healthy cells, you'd need to experiment with (genetic or pharmacological) inhibition in more realistic models of healthy cells, perhaps something patient-derived.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly identifies this as a limitation and sketches what would be needed (patient-derived samples, organoids). However, this reads more as "acknowledged limitation of the current analysis" rather than "urgent blocker I need to solve now." The author published the analysis despite this limitation, suggesting they're comfortable with it as exploratory work rather than desperately seeking validation resources.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is relevant as long as the author continues pursuing broad-spectrum cancer target identification, but the post itself is presented as a completed exploratory analysis rather than an ongoing project. The author may or may not pursue this further experimentally.
problems.source_post: cancer selective pan essential targets from depmap

problems.problem: Looking for examples of disease prodromes amenable to biomarker research
problems.description: In 'ai deep research tools reviewed', the author is specifically seeking chronic diseases with prodromes where 'more research into biomarkers or molecular signatures of a prodromal state would be especially valuable in treating/preventing the disease.' She notes that well-known precancerous conditions aren't promising for this but is looking for other examples beyond the standard neurological cases that dominate the literature.
evidence: I'm interested in areas where more research into biomarkers or molecular signatures of a prodromal state would be especially valuable in treating/preventing the disease.

my interest is in finding chronic progressive diseases that are hard to treat by the time they're diagnosed, where it might make more sense to study the prodromes to see if the underlying progressive dysfunction can be slowed/stopped
Well-known examples of precancerous conditions, which are generally handled with surgery or watchful waiting, are *mostly* not that promising for additional research
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real research need, but it's more of an information gathering phase than an urgent blocker. The author is actively seeking these examples but has approaches to find them herself. A stranger with specific domain expertise might get a read and response, but it's not blocking her work.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is part of the same active research project. The need for identifying promising prodrome cases will persist through the literature review and analysis phase, likely 3-4 months based on her stated intention to do comprehensive manual research.

problems.problem: Designing AI tools that encourage critical thinking vs. passive consumption
problems.description: In 'i vibecoded a dispute resolution app', Sarah grapples with the broader question of how to design AI tools that require users to think critically rather than encouraging them to 'turn their brains off.' She acknowledges this as a concern for her dispute resolution app but admits she doesn't have expertise in human-computer interaction and lacks clear answers.
evidence: In general, I do worry that it's bad for people to use AIs as all-knowing oracles or as an excuse to avoid thinking, and it's possible that *if* something like FairEnough were to become widely used, it would be contributing to the problem.

How *can* one design AI tools that require the user to think critically rather than subtly encouraging them to turn their brains off? I don't know; there are people thinking about this, but I'm not a human-computer interaction expert and don't really have opinions here myself.
In the meanwhile, I think personal responsibility is a good idea. Think about what you use AI for, and why, and whether you'd be better off in the long run doing that particular task on your own.
problems.email_open_reasoning: While Sarah raises this as a concern, she explicitly states she's 'not a human-computer interaction expert and don't really have opinions here myself' and defaults to 'personal responsibility' as the solution. This reads more as an intellectual acknowledgment of a design challenge than an active problem she's trying to solve or seeking help with.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a broader design philosophy question that may persist as long as she continues building AI tools, but it's not urgent since her current project is a small-scale hobby app. She may revisit this if the app scales, but that seems unlikely based on her stated intentions.
problems.source_post: i vibecoded a dispute resolution app
